name: Caterpie
route: Forest edges, shaded berry groves, abandoned orchards, damp trails, low branches, leaf litter, and protected canopy nurseries
habitat: Pesticide-free forests with broadleaf plants, soft bark, climbing surfaces, humid shade, and dense understory cover
behaviorTemperament: Usually timid and nonaggressive. Wild Caterpie freeze when seen, cling to bark with suction feet, then raise the red antenna if touched or cornered.
diet: Fresh leaves, tender shoots, fruit skin, pollen dust, soft berries, and moisture from damp foliage
activityPattern: Diurnal to crepuscular in safe forests. Nocturnal feeding increases during hot dry spells or heavy bird-predator pressure.
bestTimeToFind: Early morning after dew, on the underside of broad leaves, near fresh bite trails, silk threads, or faint sweet-rot odor
observationDifficulty: Easy to moderate. Common in healthy forests, but their color and stillness make them easy to miss unless feeding marks are visible.
populationStatus: Stable in clean forests but collapses quickly near pesticides, heavy smoke, invasive bird colonies, or over-collecting by novice trainers
description: A soft larval Bug-type Pokémon with suction feet, silk glands, and a defensive odor organ. Its body looks harmless, but it is built for one desperate task: eat fast enough to survive until metamorphosis.
lore: In old ranger stories, a silent forest with no Caterpie chewing is a warning sign. Something has poisoned the leaves, or something larger has already eaten its way through the nursery.
survivalUse: Can indicate safe edible foliage, nearby water-rich vegetation, pesticide-free habitat, and healthy pollinator cycles. Silk threads can help mark a trail in an emergency, but harvesting too much can kill the individual.
handlingWarning: Do not grab the red antenna, squeeze the body, or place Caterpie in airtight containers. The odor defense can cause nausea, eye watering, and panic in enclosed shelters.
encounterAdvice: Observe without touching. If it raises the antenna, back away and avoid breathing directly over it. Never shake branches to make one fall.
firstAid: For odor exposure, move to fresh air, rinse eyes with clean water, and wash skin with mild soap. For accidental crushing injuries, isolate the Pokémon on damp leaves and contact a Bug-type rehabilitator.
companionSuitability: Good for beginners only if handled gently and fed clean leaves daily
fieldBehaviors: Climbs vertical bark with suction feet
Releases foul odor from antenna when threatened
Molts repeatedly as it prepares to evolve
signsOfPresence: Fresh leaf margins chewed in arcs
Soft green frass pellets under feeding sites
Sweet rot mixed with bitter odor near disturbed leaves
fieldNote: A feeding Caterpie is a healthy Caterpie. A still Caterpie with a slack antenna may be cold, poisoned, parasitized, or preparing for emergency pupation.
originStory: The Silk-Hunger Lineage likely began as an ordinary leaf-eating caterpillar exposed to energy-rich forest plants and heavy bird predation. Individuals that could eat faster, climb better, and foul the air around their attackers lived long enough to seal themselves into the next stage. The dark cost is that Caterpie never truly rests; every quiet hour is stolen from the thin margin between growth and being eaten.
domesticationStatus: Commonly handled by novice trainers but still fragile
prey: Not a predator. Consumes leaves, shoots, fruit skin, and pollen residue.
territoryRange: Usually 20 to 100 ft around host plants, with short daily movements between feeding leaves and hiding stems
lifeCycle: Hatches from a small Bug-type egg, feeds rapidly through repeated molts, and evolves into Pokemon-Metapod starting at level 7.
containmentRequirements: Ventilated soft-sided enclosure, pesticide-free host leaves, climbing twigs, high but not wet humidity, no loose mesh that can tear the body, and daily waste removal
ecologicalRole: Prunes fast-growing plants, converts leaf matter into prey biomass, feeds many bird and reptile Pokémon, and supports the later pollination role of Pokemon-Butterfree.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Vulnerable to fungal bloom, parasitoid wasps, mites, bacterial gut rot, and pesticide poisoning.
reproductionNesting: Adults place eggs under host leaves in small clusters. Larvae stay near the hatching plant unless food is exhausted or predators find the patch.
predators: Pidgey, Spearow, Fearow, Beedrill, Venonat, small reptile Pokémon, spiders, humans collecting too many juveniles, and starving forest birds
emergencyResponse: If odor defense is released indoors, ventilate the area, move people with asthma away, and avoid spraying the Caterpie with chemical air freshener or insecticide.
legalStatus: Usually legal to observe or register, but collection may be restricted in protected forests and trainer nursery areas
lifespan: Wild: often days to weeks before evolution or predation. Managed care: 1 to 2 years if metamorphosis is delayed or suppressed by care conditions.
socialStructure: Loose feeding clusters. Not truly cooperative, but groups create enough odor and silk disturbance to confuse small predators.

fieldCapabilities.odorRange: Noticeable 3 to 10 ft outdoors, stronger in enclosed shelters
fieldCapabilities.silkOutput: Light draglines and anchor threads, not load-bearing for humans
fieldCapabilities.climbingAbility: Excellent on bark, leaves, and rough stone
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Slow crawl with short panic acceleration
fieldCapabilities.defensiveCloud: Antenna odor can deter predators and irritate human eyes or stomach
fieldCapabilities.camouflage: High among green leaves, low on bare ground

trainingRisk: Low physical danger but high fragility. Most injuries come from squeezing, dirty leaves, heat stress, or accidental poisoning.
physiology: Caterpie has a soft segmented body, suction-pad feet, a simple but efficient digestive tract, silk-producing glands, and an antenna-like odor organ used when threatened. The body prioritizes feeding and growth over defense, making the species biologically successful but individually disposable.

keyFacts.diet: Leaves, shoots, fruit skin, and pollen residue
keyFacts.habitat: Forests, berry groves, and shaded routes
keyFacts.lifespan: Usually short in the wild before evolution or predation
keyFacts.groupSize: Small feeding clusters on host plants

domesticationDifficulty: Easy but delicate. Care failures usually come from contaminated food, rough handling, or poor ventilation.
physicalCharacteristics: Green segmented body, yellow ring markings, large black eyes, short suction feet, a red defensive antenna, and a soft body surface that can be injured by direct pressure.
dietRestrictions: Avoid pesticide-treated leaves, dry leaves, moldy fruit, oily human food, salt, smoke residue, and plants collected near roads or fuel spills.

pokenatomyInspiration.pages: Pokemon-Caterpie anatomy and profile pages 30-31 of the uploaded Pokenatomy PDF
usedFor: prey role in forest food chains

evolutionLine.name: Pokemon-Caterpie
evolutionLine.method: Wild hatchling or egg. Evolves into Pokemon-Metapod starting at level 7.
evolutionLine.note: Soft-bodied larval stage with suction feet, silk production, and a defensive odor organ.

evolutionLine.name: Pokemon-Metapod
evolutionLine.method: Evolves from Pokemon-Caterpie starting at level 7. Evolves into Pokemon-Butterfree starting at level 10.
evolutionLine.note: Immobile chrysalis stage. The shell hardens while the body inside breaks down and rebuilds for flight.

evolutionLine.name: Pokemon-Butterfree
evolutionLine.method: Evolves from Pokemon-Metapod starting at level 10. Gigantamax form requires the Gigantamax factor.
evolutionLine.note: Final Bug/Flying pollinator stage with compound eyes, water-repellent toxic wing powder, and honey-seeking behavior.

evolutionTree.subtitle: Evolution Tree - Silk and Powder Line

mainLine.name: Pokemon-Caterpie
mainLine.note: Soft prey-stage larva that survives by eating constantly, climbing well, making silk, and releasing foul odor from its antenna.

mainLine.name: Pokemon-Metapod
mainLine.note: Chrysalis stage with little mobility. The shell is armor, but the forming body inside is dangerously tender.

mainLine.name: Pokemon-Butterfree
mainLine.note: Winged adult that carries pollen, honey, and toxic defensive powder through forest canopies and rainstorms.

target.name: Pokemon-Gigantamax-Butterfree
target.note: Rare battle phenomenon. Treat wing scales and powder clouds as area contamination hazards.

originContinuity.coreMutation: A forest caterpillar line developed hyper-efficient gut chambers, defensive scent glands, and silk scaffolding that allowed sudden, energy-heavy metamorphosis into a flying adult.
originContinuity.sharedDarkTheme: The line survives by accepting three different kinds of weakness: Caterpie is eaten by nearly everything, Metapod must become helpless while its body rebuilds, and Butterfree survives by filling the air with powder that can save it while harming everything nearby.
stageProgression: Pokemon-Caterpie: hunger is the survival engine. The larva eats until its skin can no longer contain it.
Pokemon-Metapod: the body becomes a sealed workshop. A living animal dissolves into tissue that can be rebuilt into wings.
Pokemon-Butterfree: the adult escapes the food chain by becoming airborne, toxic, and useful to forests as a pollinator.
originContinuity.fieldMyth: Old Kanto rangers call mass Metapod clusters 'green graves' because a healthy grove can look like it is full of hanging shells, and only some of them ever open from the inside.

sourceNotes: Official Pokémon Pokédex data used for typing, category, height, weight, weakness, ability, descriptions, and evolution family.
PokémonDB data used for level-based evolution, hidden abilities, base stats, breeding data, and detailed type defenses.
Pokenatomy: An Unofficial Guide used as dark anatomical inspiration for Caterpie silk and odor organs, Metapod chrysalis vulnerability, and Butterfree toxic wing powder and pollination behavior.
Dark origin material is original fiction created for the Field Pokédex and is not official Pokémon canon.
Naming convention uses Pokemon-XXX for file names, ids, detailPath values, index names, and evolution node names. displayName and commonName keep the readable Pokémon name.
